content.thinking:
Now I can see the current state:

**Current permissions on /app:**
- admin: `.*`, `.*`, `.*` (full - fine)
- ops-user: `.*`, `.*`, `.*` (full - WRONG, ops-user should not be on /app)
- app-user: `` (empty configure!), `.*`, `.*` (missing configure - BROKEN)

**Current permissions on /ops:**
- admin: `.*`, `.*`, `.*` (full - fine)
- No ops-user permissions at all! (WRONG, ops-user should have full permissions on /ops)
So I need to fix three things: grant app-user the missing configure permission on /app, revoke ops-user's access to /app entirely, and give ops-user full permissions on /ops. I'll also need to check what the client deployments are doing to understand the connection issues.

Executing the permission fixes...

content.text:
Current state is clear:
- **`/app`**: `app-user` has empty configure (broken), `ops-user` has full access (wrong vhost)
- **`/ops`**: `ops-user` has NO permissions at all (missing entirely)

I'll fix all three issues now.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-ha-clear-upgrade-01-20-22f13321-cluster-a"

# 1. Fix app-user on /app: restore the missing configure permission
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /app app-user ".*" ".*" ".*"

# 2. Remove ops-user from /app (wrong vhost)
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl clear_permissions -p /app ops-user

# 3. Grant ops-user full permissions on /ops (currently missing)
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /ops ops-user ".*" ".*" ".*"
input.description: Fix RabbitMQ user permissions on both vhosts

description: Fix RabbitMQ user permissions on both vhosts

summary: Fix RabbitMQ user permissions on both vhosts

content.text: Permissions are correct. Now I'll wait for the client deployments to recover.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-ha-clear-upgrade-01-20-22f13321-cluster-a"
# Restart the crashing deployments so they pick up immediately
kubectl -n $NS rollout restart deployment/app-client deployment/ops-client
input.description: Restart client deployments to reconnect with fixed permissions

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-ha-clear-upgrade-01-20-22f13321-cluster-a"
for i in $(seq 1 12); do
  echo "--- attempt $i ---"
  kubectl -n $NS get pods --no-headers | grep -E 'app-client|ops-client|rabbitmq'
  READY=$(kubectl -n $NS get pods --no-headers | grep -E 'app-client|ops-client' | awk '{print $2}' | grep -v "1/1" | wc -l)
  if [ "$READY" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Both client pods are healthy!"
    break
  fi
  sleep 10
done
input.description: Poll until both client deployments are running and healthy

description: Poll until both client deployments are running and healthy

content.thinking: `ops-client` is now running fine. But `app-client` is still crashing. Let me investigate the app-client logs to see what's going wrong.

content.text: `ops-client` is healthy but `app-client` is still crashing. Let me investigate its logs.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-ha-clear-upgrade-01-20-22f13321-cluster-a"
kubectl -n $NS logs deployment/app-client --tail=50 2>&1 || true
echo "--- previous pod logs ---"
kubectl -n $NS logs deployment/app-client --previous --tail=50 2>&1 || true
input.description: Get app-client logs to diagnose crash

content.thinking:
The app-client is failing because of a queue argument mismatch: the `app-queue` already exists with `x-queue-mode: lazy`, but the app-client is trying to declare it without that argument. The app-client can't redeclare the queue with different arguments.

I need to delete and recreate the queue with the right arguments. Let me check the current queue state and what the app-client expects.

Options:
1. Delete the existing `app-queue` and let the app-client recreate it
2. Use the RabbitMQ management API to delete the queue
I'll delete the queue through the management API or rabbitmqctl command.

content.text: The app-client fails because `app-queue` already exists with `x-queue-mode: lazy` but the client tries to declare it without that argument. I need to delete the queue so the client can recreate it correctly.

input.command:
NS="karma-rabbitmq-ha-clear-upgrade-01-20-22f13321-cluster-a"
# Check what queues exist and their properties
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl list_queues -p /app name arguments durable
